Q1: My study routine was honestly a bit inconsistent, but I made it work for me. I'd usually carve out about 8 hours a week, mostly in the evenings after work or other commitments. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time going through written materials and making my own notes. Some weeks I'd be more structured, other weeks it was more scattered depending on what was due. I tried to stay on top of things but I won't pretend I had a perfect schedule.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. When assignments were coming up, I'd buckle down and study more intensively. During quieter weeks, I might spread things out more casually. Being 24 and juggling various responsibilities, I couldn't always stick to a rigid timetable. I learned to be flexible and adapt, which actually helped me stay resilient when things got hectic.
Q3: Usually I'd log into the LMS when I knew there was new content posted or when I needed to check on assignment details. Sometimes I'd get email notifications that would prompt me to jump in. I also made it a habit to check in a few times a week just to make sure I wasn't missing anything important. The anxiety about falling behind was real, so logging in regularly helped ease that worry a bit.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks, logging into the LMS became pretty automatic for me. I'd usually check it in the morning with my coffee and then again in the evening. It became part of my daily rhythm, almost like checking my email or social media. Having that routine helped manage my anxiety because I always knew where I stood with the course materials and deadlines.
Q5: The written resources and lecture notes were gold for me since I learn best through reading and writing. I also found the discussion forums somewhat helpful for getting different perspectives, even though I only posted a couple of times myself. The assignment guidelines were crucial because they helped me understand exactly what was expected. Video content was useful too, though I didn't watch as much as I probably should have.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't watch the videos as thoroughly as I could have—I'd often skim through them or watch at higher speed. Some of the optional supplementary readings I skipped entirely when time was tight. The peer interaction features I used selectively; I engaged with about 20 peers but ignored a lot of the broader discussion threads. When my anxiety was high, I'd focus only on what seemed absolutely essential.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to assignments or exams. If I could immediately apply what I was learning or if it helped clarify something I was confused about, that made it valuable. I also appreciated activities that let me work at my own pace since I could fit them around my schedule. Anything that felt like busywork without clear purpose got pushed to the bottom of my list.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—there was less pressure but also less motivation to complete them. Required activities had that deadline urgency that pushed me to engage even when I didn't feel like it. With optional stuff, I'd often think 'I'll get to it later' and then never did. My anxiety actually worked in my favor with required tasks because the fear of missing deadlines kept me moving forward.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge chunk of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, then work through them on my tablet or print them out to annotate. I kept a physical notebook where I'd rewrite key concepts in my own words—that's how I really learn. I also used external resources like YouTube tutorials and academic articles I found through Google Scholar. The LMS was just the starting point, not the whole picture.
Q10: There were many times when I'd download a week's worth of materials in one quick session, then spend hours studying them offline over the next few days. The LMS would only show that brief download activity, not the three hours I spent later working through everything with my notes. Same with exam prep—I'd review my handwritten notes and past assignments without ever touching the LMS. My actual learning time was probably double what the system recorded.
Q11: All the time, honestly. I'd access the reading materials once, download everything I needed, and then work through them on my own terms. Sometimes I'd revisit the LMS to check if there were updates, but the actual studying happened with my downloaded files. This was especially true for longer documents that I wanted to annotate and highlight. My offline study sessions were where the real deep learning happened.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, particularly around midterms when everything seemed to pile up at once. My anxiety would spike during those times, making it harder to focus even when I was sitting with the materials. Sometimes personal stuff would interfere too—being 24, I've got responsibilities outside of school that don't pause for coursework. The mindfulness exercises I learned actually helped me push through those difficult periods.
Q13: Fatigue was probably my biggest enemy—after a long day, the last thing I wanted was to log into the LMS and engage with dense material. When I was tired or overwhelmed, I'd do the bare minimum online and save the real studying for when I had more mental energy. Competing responsibilities definitely affected my timing; I'd sometimes cram LMS activities into whatever gaps I could find. Confusion about certain topics would make me avoid those sections until I absolutely had to face them.
Q14: Yeah, there were times when I'd be scrolling through materials but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd have the LMS open, maybe watching a video or reading, but I wasn't actually absorbing anything. This usually happened when my anxiety was particularly high or when I was stressed about other things. I'd have to go back later and re-engage with the same content when I was in a better headspace.
Q15: I definitely postponed some assignments even when I knew they were important. My assignment completion rate wasn't great—I'll admit that. Sometimes the anxiety about doing something perfectly would make me avoid starting it at all. Other times, I'd prioritize what seemed most urgent and let other things slide. The irony is that avoiding things usually made my anxiety worse, but in the moment it felt like self-preservation.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. Without them, I'd probably drift and procrastinate endlessly. When a deadline approached, my LMS activity would spike dramatically—I'd be checking requirements, reviewing materials, engaging with whatever I needed. I worked best under that pressure, even though it wasn't the healthiest approach. The structure deadlines provided actually helped channel my anxiety into productivity.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced my engagement, especially when it was constructive and showed me how to improve. Good feedback on an assignment would motivate me to put more effort into the next one. When feedback was delayed or vague, I'd feel uncertain about whether I was on the right track, which fed into my anxiety. I appreciated instructors who gave timely responses because it helped me adjust my approach.
Q18: The course structure with its 18 online components gave me flexibility, which I needed given my schedule. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me plan my time, even if I didn't always stick to the plan perfectly. The structure was supportive enough that I could work independently but still know what was expected. When the structure was clear, my anxiety was more manageable because I knew exactly what I needed to do.
Q19: The availability of downloadable resources made engagement much easier for me since I could study offline. Clear assignment guidelines and rubrics reduced my anxiety about expectations. What made things harder was when content felt scattered across different sections of the LMS—I'd waste time just finding what I needed. Also, when video content was the primary format without text alternatives, that didn't suit my learning style as well.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to engage but struggling to produce quality work. They might be reading, watching, exploring—but when it comes to assignments, anxiety or confusion holds them back. It could also be someone who's overwhelmed and using the LMS as a way to feel productive without tackling the harder tasks. Sometimes high activity is actually avoidance behavior dressed up as engagement.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some extent. My exam score was decent and I got a Merit, but my LMS activity probably didn't tell the whole story. A lot of my effective studying happened offline with downloaded materials and my own notes. Someone could appear minimally active in the system while actually putting in serious work elsewhere. The LMS captures presence but not necessarily the quality or depth of learning.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of access—when someone logs in, what they click on, how often they engage with the platform. They can track submission times and participation in discussions. But they completely miss offline study, the quality of attention during online sessions, and emotional engagement with the material. They can't capture whether someone is genuinely learning or just going through the motions. The numbers tell a partial story at best.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what I'm learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's wrestling with concepts, making connections, and applying what I learn. For me, engagement also means managing my anxiety well enough to stay focused and resilient when things get tough. True engagement is active and intentional, not passive consumption.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show all the time I spent with downloaded materials, my handwritten notes, or external resources I used to supplement my learning. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness practices I used to manage my anxiety so I could actually focus on studying. It also wouldn't show the mental effort I put into understanding difficult concepts or the resilience it took to keep going when things felt overwhelming. My engagement was much deeper than any login data could reflect.
